caveats: a: The occupational source covers NAICS 812300 rather than only 812332.
a: Industry technology examples establish feasibility and current baseline, not a measured task share.

rho: The estimate separates incremental AI realization from mechanical automation already in place.

e: Mixed-service operators can be classified inconsistently across adjacent laundry codes.

d5: Real output is not identical to demand faced by a representative lower-middle-market operator.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring business-to-business rental or service contracts combining textile inventory, scheduled laundering, replacement, and route delivery; ancillary revenue can include loss charges, special processing, and related facility-service items.
lens.excluded_activities: Consumer coin laundries and dry cleaners, linen-only supply services classified elsewhere, captive in-house laundries, textile manufacturing, and firms outside the lower-middle-market operating-company population.
lens.included_activities: U.S. industrial laundries supplying laundered work uniforms, protective and clean-room apparel, and dust-control textiles on rental or contract service routes, including washing, finishing, inventory control, pickup, and delivery.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader NAICS 812300 workforce as predominantly production and transport/material-moving occupations, while the industry association describes substantial existing mechanical automation in wash aisles and soil sorting. I treat the administrative, routing, inspection, forecasting, and maintenance-planning share that remains as the automatable fraction. The broader occupational group includes consumer laundries and press-heavy dry cleaning, which may overstate manual finishing relative to industrial plants; existing industrial automation may also cause this estimate to overstate incremental opportunity.
caveats: The occupational source covers NAICS 812300 rather than only 812332.
Industry technology examples establish feasibility and current baseline, not a measured task share.
a.change_evidence: An exact-code time-and-motion study separating already automated plant work from remaining clerical, inspection, dispatch, maintenance, and route tasks would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The core service still requires receiving and sorting soiled goods, operating process equipment, finishing, inventory handling, and physical route delivery. Additional software and AI can affect demand forecasting, route and production scheduling, visual quality control, customer service, billing, preventive maintenance, and some sorting decisions, but cannot perform most textile movement or delivery without further capital equipment.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for the broader NAICS 812300 laundry and dry-cleaning industry to grow from $29.4 billion in 2024 to $34.5 billion in 2034, about 1.6% annually, while employment grows only 1.9% over the decade. I translate the real-output path to roughly five years and center below its approximately 1.08 multiplier because industrial contract laundry can differ from consumer laundry. The broader category's consumer and linen mix can bias the result in either direction.
caveats: The BLS projection aggregates industrial, consumer, and other laundry services.
Real output is not identical to demand faced by a representative lower-middle-market operator.
d5.change_evidence: Exact-code inflation-adjusted revenue, pounds processed, route stops, contract retention, and end-market forecasts over a full cycle would materially change the multiplier.
d5.rationale: Uniform, protective-apparel, clean-room, and dust-control needs should persist with customer employment and facility activity. Outsourcing and route density can support volume, while customer closures, reusable-textile substitution, disposables, efficiency, and cyclical industrial exposure can offset it; the central case is modest real expansion rather than rapid growth.

caveats: The industry definition does not measure ownership independence or transaction readiness.
Mixed-service operators can be classified inconsistently across adjacent laundry codes.
e.change_evidence: Exact-code transaction screening or establishment microdata showing independent ownership, revenue, route density, customer concentration, and stand-alone transferability would materially change the range.
e.rationale: The official definition describes a route-based rental or contract service with tangible inventory and plant assets, a coherent operating company model that is usually separable and transferable. The discount allows for captive operations, mixed-code linen businesses, customer concentration, weak route density, environmental liabilities, obsolete plants, or entities that are too integrated or too small despite appearing in the industry population.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 812332 ($4,133,769,162) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 812332 ($12,258,747,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[81]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $181,203 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 812332); class <5: 81 firms, avg revenue/firm $338,272 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $52,940 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $756,244 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $118,352 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 36 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,104,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $172,802 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 104 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,149,365 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $805,876 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $22,444,833 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,512,616 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $508,540,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $79,586,577 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 42

caveats: Obligation differs across ordinary uniforms, protective apparel, clean-room garments, and dust-control products.
A high value reflects persistence of the physical service, not immunity from price or volume pressure.
o.change_evidence: End-customer surveys and contract data quantifying insourcing, disposables, customer-owned textile programs, and service cancellation by segment would materially change the range.
o.rationale: Customers require physical collection, hygienic or controlled laundering, textile inventory, replacement, and delivery; protective and clean-room garments can add documentation and quality accountability. Software cannot substitute for that operating chain. Residual substitution risk comes from customer insourcing, disposable products, customer-owned goods, alternative uniform policies, or automated equipment that changes the provider's labor model without eliminating the service.

caveats: No exact-code study directly measures retained economics from AI or advanced software.
Capture varies with local route density, contract duration, and customer bargaining power.
q.change_evidence: Matched customer-contract and plant-level data showing realized technology savings, repricing at renewal, churn, service improvements, and required capital spending would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Recurring rental and service contracts can preserve part of an operating improvement between renewals and across customers, particularly when better routing, inventory accuracy, quality, or uptime improves service rather than only cutting price. Capture is limited by competitive rebids, customer procurement, service-level commitments, wage and utility pressure, equipment and integration costs, and the modest share of total work susceptible to software-led improvement.

caveats: No representative exact-code adoption or realized-benefit study was located.
The estimate separates incremental AI realization from mechanical automation already in place.
rho.change_evidence: A representative exact-code panel reporting deployed AI use cases, implementation costs, adoption time, and realized labor or throughput gains would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Industrial laundries have a credible implementation path because route, plant, inventory, RFID, billing, and customer data can be integrated, and vendors already automate adjacent plant workflows. Realization is constrained by legacy equipment, fragmented data, site-by-site integration, capital cycles, validation requirements for protective or clean-room goods, and the fact that many gains require hardware and process redesign rather than software alone.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale-or-transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners across industries: 22% planned to sell or transfer. I use that as an upper anchor and reduce it for completed market availability, since intentions do not equal executed sales. Industrial laundry plants, routes, contracts, and textile inventories are comparatively transferable, which limits the downward adjustment, but the national cross-industry sample may still misstate this industry's age, consolidation, and succession profile in either direction.
caveats: The proxy is cross-industry and measures intentions rather than completed transactions.
Private add-on acquisitions and family transfers are incompletely visible in public data.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of exact-code owner transitions and completed control transactions, including withdrawn processes and strategic acquisitions, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Recurring routes and physical operating assets support transferability, yet only a minority of owners are likely to bring a business to market within five years. Failed succession, valuation gaps, concentration, environmental diligence, financing, and strategic-buyer competition reduce completed availability below stated owner intentions.

principal_driver: Persistent physical service obligations combined with recurring routes and contracts.
principal_weakness: Only a limited share of total work is susceptible to incremental software-led automation, and existing plant automation narrows the remaining opportunity.
